title: Rust on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; oxalic acid bounded; NO chlorine bleach (sets rust); wool = pro
stainChemistry: Rust on carpet is iron oxide bonded to the fiber — usually from a metal furniture leg, a leaky planter, or a rusty tool dropped on the carpet. The recurring traps: chlorine bleach (LOCKS rust into a permanent darker stain — chlorine + iron is the classic irreversible failure), 3% peroxide (oxidizes iron further, makes rust WORSE), and hot water (sets iron-fiber bond). The right tool is REDUCING chemistry — oxalic acid (Bar Keepers Friend, Iron Out, Whink Rust Stain Remover) which reduces Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺ and makes the iron water-soluble. Wool carpet narrows the bench sharply (oxalic acid weakens wool keratin) — pro from minute one.
whyThisWorks: Oxalic acid (BKF, Iron Out, Whink) is the reducing chemistry that pulls iron oxide off carpet fibers. It's the OPPOSITE of bleach — bleach oxidizes (locks rust), oxalic acid reduces (releases rust). On synthetic carpet (nylon, polyester, olefin), bounded oxalic acid + cool rinse + weighted towels is the home protocol. Wool carpet goes to pro because oxalic acid weakens wool keratin. Honest cap: aged set rust with deep penetration sometimes plateaus.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): rust is Fe³⁺ iron oxide bonded to cellulose / synthetic fiber. Pro uses oxalic acid (Bar Keepers Friend, Iron Out, Whink) at controlled dilution + cool rinse + acetic acid neutralization. Wool carpet requires specialty wool-safe iron-removers; oxalic damages keratin.
defaultAssumption: Rust spot from metal furniture leg, leaky planter, rusty tool, or wet metal contact on synthetic home carpet. Wool carpet = pro from minute one. Aged set rust = honest cap; pro chemistry available.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Wool carpet = pro from minute one. Synthetic carpet (nylon / polyester / olefin) = home candidate. Gloves on — oxalic acid is a skin irritant.

spottingProtocol.equipment: BKF or Iron Out per label on Q-tip
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hidden carpet area: dab oxalic acid solution on white cloth, press 30 sec. Color transfers = skip oxalic, pro for residual rust.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Bar Keepers Friend / Iron Out / Whink per label, gloves
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix oxalic acid product per label (BKF as paste with water; Iron Out as liquid). Apply with damp cloth — never pour. 5–10 min. Cool damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY (oxalic acid is a skin irritant, residue must be rinsed).

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cool water, paper towels + heavy book
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool damp-cloth rinse 2–3 passes. Weighted paper towels overnight. Fan-dry. Inspect dry.

steps: 1. Wool carpet = pro from minute one.
2. Gloves + hidden-area oxalic acid test on dyed carpet.
3. Oxalic acid pretreat (BKF / Iron Out / Whink) per label, damp-cloth application, 5–10 min.
4. Cool damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY (oxalic acid skin irritant).
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets iron-fiber bond
warnings: Wool carpet = pro from minute one — oxalic acid weakens wool
NEVER chlorine bleach on rust — LOCKS rust into permanent darker stain
NEVER 3% peroxide on rust — oxidizes iron further, makes worse
NEVER hot water — sets iron-fiber bond
Oxalic acid is a skin irritant — gloves required, rinse thoroughly
NEVER mix oxalic acid with chlorine bleach — toxic chlorine gas
Hidden test on dyed carpet before oxalic acid

homeSolutions.agent: Reducing Chemistry, Not Oxidizing — Bleach BANNED
homeSolutions.instruction: Rust on carpet is iron oxide bonded to the fiber. The most common home failure is reaching for chlorine bleach — chlorine + iron LOCKS rust into a permanent darker stain (irreversible). 3% peroxide is the second-most-common failure — peroxide oxidizes the iron further and makes rust WORSE, not better. The right tool is REDUCING chemistry: oxalic acid (Bar Keepers Friend, Iron Out, or Whink) which pulls iron off the fiber. Two hard rules: NO chlorine bleach (locks rust), NO peroxide (makes rust worse). Wool carpet goes to pro from minute one.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool Carpet Goes to Pro from Minute One
homeSolutions.instruction: Oxalic acid weakens wool keratin. If your carpet is wool, this is a pro job from the start. Synthetic carpet (nylon, polyester, olefin) is the home protocol candidate.

Disposable gloves before any oxalic acid handling — it's a skin irritant. In a hidden carpet area, dab Bar Keepers Friend paste or Iron Out solution on a white cloth, press 30 seconds. Color transfers = skip oxalic, pro for residual rust.

homeSolutions.agent: Bar Keepers Friend or Iron Out — Damp-Cloth Application
homeSolutions.instruction: Bar Keepers Friend (powder, mix to paste with water per label) OR Iron Out (liquid per label) OR Whink Rust Stain Remover. Apply with a damp cloth — NEVER pour on carpet. Press 5 to 10 minutes. Cool damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY — oxalic acid is a skin irritant and the residue needs to come out.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool Rinse + Weighted Paper Towels Overnight
homeSolutions.instruction: Cool damp-cloth rinse 2 to 3 passes. Stack paper towels on the spot, weight overnight. Fan-dry in the morning. Pulls residual moisture and oxalic out of the pile and prevents brown wicking back from the pad.

Aged rust that's been on the carpet for weeks or months sometimes plateaus even after the full sequence. Pro auto-detailers and specialty carpet cleaners have specialty iron-removers home users can't safely access. For valuable carpet, escalate sooner.

steps: 1. Wool carpet = pro from minute one. NO chlorine bleach. NO peroxide.
2. Gloves on (oxalic acid is skin irritant).
4. Bar Keepers Friend / Iron Out / Whink per label, damp-cloth application, 5–10 min.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on wool carpet (pro). Stop if hidden-area test shows carpet color loss. Stop if chlorine bleach or peroxide was already used (locked / made worse — irreversible, pro escalation). Stop after 2 home cycles plateau.
warnings: Wool carpet = pro from minute one
NEVER chlorine bleach on rust — LOCKS rust permanently
NEVER 3% peroxide on rust — makes rust WORSE
NEVER hot water — sets iron-fiber bond
Oxalic acid is skin irritant — gloves + thorough rinse
NEVER mix oxalic acid with chlorine — toxic chlorine gas

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on rust — LOCKS rust permanently
Never 3% peroxide on rust — oxidizes iron further, makes worse
Never hot water on rust — sets iron-fiber bond
Never mix oxalic acid with chlorine bleach — toxic chlorine gas
Never bare-handed with oxalic acid — skin irritant
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet: tolerates oxalic acid (BKF / Iron Out / Whink)
Wool carpet: pro from minute one — oxalic acid weakens wool keratin
Dyed carpet: hidden test mandatory before oxalic
Iron oxide is a REDUCTION chemistry stain — opposite of most stains

materialWarnings: Bleach LOCKS rust permanently — most common irreversible failure
Peroxide makes rust WORSE — second-most-common failure
Reducing chemistry (oxalic acid) is the right tool
Wool carpet = pro from minute one

escalation.when: Wool carpet from minute one. Chlorine bleach already used (locked rust permanently). Peroxide already used (made rust worse). Hidden-area test shows carpet color loss. 2 home cycles plateau. Aged set rust with deep penetration.
escalation.whatToTell: "Rust on [synthetic / wool] carpet, source [furniture leg / planter / metal tool], age [fresh / weeks / months], home-treated with [Bar Keepers Friend / Iron Out per label damp-cloth + cool rinse + weighted towels]. NO chlorine bleach, NO peroxide applied. [Residual rust / locked by chlorine / oxidized by peroxide]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with iron-removal capability; specialty wool carpet cleaner if wool

contextQuestions: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet or wool?
Source of rust — furniture leg, planter, tool, water?
Has chlorine bleach or peroxide been applied (often irreversible)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Rust is the rare REDUCTION chemistry stain — the opposite of most stains. Bleach and peroxide both make it worse, not better. Oxalic acid (the active in BKF, Iron Out, Whink) is the right tool. Wool carpet adds the keratin-damage risk that puts the whole protocol in pro territory.
